content.problemStatement:
Chef has a rooted tree **G**, consisting of **N** vertices. Each edge of the tree has a color black or white.

Once Chef's friend Ksen offered Chef to play a game on the tree **G**. The game has very simple rule:



The players make moves in the game alternately.
On his move Chef deletes a single undeleted black edge. On her move Ksen deletes a single undeleted white edge.
When some edge has been deleted, all the edges that now aren't connected (directly or indirectly) to the root will be deleted too.
One who cannot move lose the game.


Chef really likes the game. The only problem is that Ksen knows the game very well. So she always plays optimally. That's why Chef has decided to choose the order of moves. Help Chef: tell him who will win the game if he moves first, and also who will win the game if Ksen moves first. Consider that Chef plays optimally too.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N, followed by N-1 lines, each containing three integers Ui, Vi, Ci, representing the current edge of the tree.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print two strings: the name of the winner if Chef moves first, and the name of the winner if Ksen moves first.

sampleTestCases.output:
Ksen Ksen
Chef Ksen
Ksen Ksen
Ksen Chef
Chef Chef
sampleTestCases.explanation: The first test case has only one edge, which is black. Therefore, Ksen wins if Chef moves first, and Ksen wins if Ksen moves first. The second test case has two edges, one black and one white. Therefore, Chef wins if Chef moves first, and Ksen wins if Ksen moves first. The third test case has three edges, two black and one white. Therefore, Ksen wins if Chef moves first, and Ksen wins if Ksen moves first. The fourth test case has three edges, two black and one white. Therefore, Ksen wins if Chef moves first, and Chef wins if Ksen moves first. The fifth test case has three edges, two black and one white. Therefore, Chef wins if Chef moves first, and Chef wins if Ksen moves first.
The test case has four edges, three black and one white. Therefore, Ksen wins if Chef moves first, and Ksen wins if Ksen moves first.
The test case has five edges, four black and one white. Therefore, Chef wins if Chef moves first, and Chef wins if Ksen moves first.

hidden.label: stress/general
hidden.expectedOutput:
Ksen Ksen
Chef Chef
... (10 test cases)

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a depth-first search (DFS) to traverse the tree and count the number of black and white edges. We can then use the Nim theory to determine the winner of the game.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, O(T * N) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the tree.
